title: Urinary Incontinence: Types and Treatment

sections.title: Classification
sections.content:
**Stress Urinary Incontinence (SUI):**
• Leakage with cough, sneeze, exertion
• Urethral hypermobility or ISD
• More common in women

**Urgency Urinary Incontinence (UUI):**
• Leakage with sudden urge
• Overactive bladder component
• Can be idiopathic or neurogenic

**Mixed Incontinence:**
• Both stress and urgency components
• Very common

**Overflow Incontinence:**
• Continuous dribbling
• Incomplete emptying
• BPH, neurogenic bladder

sections.title: Evaluation
sections.content:
**History:**
• Voiding diary
• Symptom questionnaires
• Pad usage
• Impact on quality of life

**Physical Exam:**
• Pelvic exam (prolapse, atrophy)
• Cough stress test
• PVR measurement
• Neurologic exam

**Basic Workup:**
• Urinalysis (rule out UTI)
• PVR

**Advanced (if complex):**
• Urodynamics
• Cystoscopy

sections.title: Treatment - Conservative
sections.content:
**Behavioral:**
• Pelvic floor exercises (Kegels)
• Bladder training
• Timed voiding
• Fluid management
• Weight loss

**Physical Therapy:**
• Pelvic floor PT
• Biofeedback
• Very effective first-line

**Devices:**
• Pessary for SUI/prolapse
• Urethral inserts

**Absorbent Products:**
• Pads, protective underwear
• For management while treating

sections.title: Treatment - Medical
sections.content:
**For Urgency/OAB:**
• Anticholinergics (oxybutynin, solifenacin)
• Beta-3 agonists (mirabegron, vibegron)
• Topical estrogen (postmenopausal)

**Side Effects of Anticholinergics:**
• Dry mouth, constipation
• Cognitive impairment (avoid in elderly)
• Urinary retention

**For Stress Incontinence:**
• No approved medications
• Duloxetine used off-label (not in US)
• Focus on PT and surgery

sections.title: Treatment - Surgical
sections.content:
**For Stress Incontinence:**
• Midurethral sling (TVT, TOT)
  - Gold standard
  - 80-90% success
• Burch colposuspension
• Urethral bulking agents

**For Urgency Incontinence:**
• Sacral neuromodulation (InterStim)
• Percutaneous tibial nerve stimulation
• Botulinum toxin injection
• Augmentation cystoplasty (rare)

**Artificial Urinary Sphincter:**
• For intrinsic sphincter deficiency
• Post-prostatectomy incontinence
• Neurogenic incontinence

keyTakeaways: Classify incontinence by type before treating
Behavioral therapy and PT are first-line for all types
Anticholinergics for urgency, surgery for stress
Avoid anticholinergics in elderly if possible
Midurethral sling is gold standard for stress incontinence

reviewQuestions.question: A 55-year-old woman leaks urine when coughing or exercising. She has tried pelvic floor PT for 3 months without improvement. What is the most appropriate next step?
reviewQuestions.explanation: Before proceeding to surgery for stress incontinence, urodynamic study confirms the diagnosis and rules out other contributing factors. If confirmed as SUI with failed conservative therapy, midurethral sling would be appropriate.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected urinary incontinence?

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in urinary incontinence. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.
Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.
Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of urinary incontinence. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.
Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.
While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

mcqs.stem: When managing urinary incontinence, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.
